summary:
Isaac Newton's Philosophiae Naturalis Principia Mathematica ('Mathematical Principles of Natural Philosophy', 1687) was arguably the most important scientific work ever published — a systematic mathematical treatment of mechanics and gravitation that synthesized the work of [[event:2362|Galileo]], [[event:2474|Kepler]], and Descartes into a unified framework of natural laws governing all physical motion, from falling apples to planetary orbits, and established the mechanistic worldview that dominated physics for the next two centuries. Newton's three laws of motion (inertia, F=ma, action-reaction) and his law of universal gravitation (gravitational force is proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance) explained an extraordinary range of phenomena: the elliptical orbits of planets (explaining Kepler's laws from first principles), the trajectory of projectiles, tidal patterns, the precession of the equinoxes, and the orbits of comets.

The Principia's intellectual impact extended far beyond physics. Its demonstration that the universe operated according to mathematically expressible, universally applicable natural laws — accessible to reason and verifiable by observation — provided the [[event:104|Scientific Revolution's]] culminating achievement and the conceptual foundation for the [[event:2346|Enlightenment]]. If nature operated by rational laws, then perhaps human society could too — Newton's achievement inspired attempts to apply mathematical rationalism to economics, politics, and ethics. Newton (simultaneously with [[event:3289|Leibniz]]) had also developed calculus — the mathematical tool essential for expressing his laws — creating the language in which modern physics is still written. Voltaire popularized Newton's ideas in France through his Lettres philosophiques (1733); the Principia became the defining text of the [[event:104|Scientific Revolution]] and established the standard — mathematical precision, experimental verification, universal applicability — that subsequent science would try to emulate.

read_more_links.title: Newton three laws motion universal gravitation
